text:
The fix is in place. I couldn't execute the runtime check — `node` execution is gated behind an approval I can't grant from here, and three attempts (including with the sandbox override) all returned "requires approval." So the result below is from tracing the code by hand, not from a run.

The one-line change on `ring_buffer.js:5`:

```js
push(x) { buf[(head + size) % cap] = x; if (size < cap) size++; else head = (head + 1) % cap; },
```

When the buffer isn't full, it grows `size` as before. Once full (`size === cap`), each push still overwrites the oldest slot at `(head + size) % cap` — which equals `head` — and now also advances `head = (head + 1) % cap` so the pointer tracks the new oldest element. The implicit tail, `(head + size) % cap`, therefore moves forward in lockstep on every overflow, which is the head/tail state the test pins.

Hand trace, capacity 3: after pushing 1,2,3 → `[1,2,3]` (head 0); push 4 → `[2,3,4]` (head 1); push 5,6 → `[4,5,6]` (head 0). Capacity 4 pushing 1..10 → `[7,8,9,10]`. The behavior is general — it's plain modular arithmetic over `cap`, with no input-specific branching.

Decision trail: chose to advance `head` only in the `else` (overflow) branch rather than recomputing head/tail from a write counter, because it's the minimal change that fixes the stated bug and matches the existing `head`/`size` representation. Verification by manual trace only — the `node` runtime check is unverified because every `Bash` invocation was blocked by an approval prompt; rerun the trace script above to confirm against the actual test's pinned head/tail.
